rationale: §8a Abs.1 Satz 1 obliges KRITIS operators to take appropriate (angemessene) organisational and technical precautions; ISO 27005 Cl.5 defines the overarching iterative information security risk management framework within which 'appropriateness' is determined, but BSIG also covers non-risk-process governance obligations not in 27005.

§8a Abs.1 Satz 2 specifies four protection goals — Verfügbarkeit, Integrität, Authentizität, Vertraulichkeit (VAIC) — as the impact dimensions for assessing disruptions; ISO 27005 Cl.6.4.3.2 requires definition of consequence criteria aligned to exactly the same CIA(A) security properties as measurable impact dimensions.

§8a Abs.1 Satz 2 requires measures to meet 'state of the art' (Stand der Technik); ISO 27005 Cl.6.2 requires capturing requirements of interested parties including regulatory obligations as risk-context inputs, which operationalises the state-of-the-art obligation as a context constraint, though 27005 does not itself define or mandate state-of-the-art as a benchmark.

§8a Abs.1 Satz 2 requires proportionate (angemessene) measures calibrated to the risk; ISO 27005 Cl.6.3 directly operationalises proportionality by requiring establishment and maintenance of risk acceptance criteria and assessment criteria, providing the yardstick for 'angemessen'.

§8a Abs.1 Satz 2 elevates Verfügbarkeit as a primary protection goal specific to critical infrastructure service continuity; ISO 27005 Cl.6.4.3.2 includes availability within the broader CIA consequence criteria framework — the KRITIS emphasis on availability as a primary goal is a sector-specific narrowing of the 27005 multi-CIA impact model.

§8a Abs.1 Satz 2 names Integrität as a protected property; ISO 27005 Cl.6.4.3.2 includes integrity within consequence criteria — the BSIG naming of individual CIA dimensions is a policy-level restatement of what 27005 requires as assessable consequence criteria.

§8a Abs.1 Satz 2 explicitly mandates Authentizität as a fourth protection goal beyond the standard CIA triad; ISO 27005 Cl.6.4.3.2 focuses on CIA consequence criteria and does not explicitly enumerate authenticity as a distinct dimension — BSIG thus broadens the standard impact scope.

§8a Abs.1 Satz 2 names Vertraulichkeit as a protected property; ISO 27005 Cl.6.4.3.2 includes confidentiality within consequence criteria — the BSIG naming is a policy-level restatement of what 27005 requires as measurable impact dimension.

§8a Abs.1 scopes obligations to IT systems, components and processes of KRITIS installations as defined in KritisV; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 requires establishing the organisational context including ISMS scope, assets and stakeholders — the statutory scope definition is a sub-element of the broader context-establishment activity.

§8a Abs.1 requires organisational precautions (e.g. ISMS governance, policies, roles); ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 covers the risk treatment process including selection of controls, which includes organisational measures — but 8.6 is risk-treatment-centric while §8a organisational precautions also encompass management-system requirements beyond treatment.

§8a Abs.1 requires technical precautions as risk-proportionate security measures; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 identifies, selects and plans risk treatment options including technical controls — direct mapping for the selection/planning phase but BSIG also covers implementation obligations that 27005 delegates to ISO 27001 Cl.8.3.

§8a Abs.1 implicitly mandates risk analysis as the basis for determining 'angemessene' measures (confirmed in BSI KdA and § 8a Abs.3 audit requirement for risk analysis); ISO 27005 Cl.7 (Information security risk assessment process) directly specifies the identification, analysis and evaluation process that substantiates proportionality of measures.

§8a Abs.1 proportionality obligation requires identifying risks to KRITIS installations; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 (risk identification — confirmed by PECB and project usage as covering event-based and asset-based risk identification) is the direct process step — sub-clause number consistent with project convention but not independently verified against official text. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

§8a Abs.1 requires measures proportionate to assessed risk, implying quantitative or qualitative risk analysis (likelihood × impact); ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 (risk analysis — likelihood and consequence estimation, qualitative/semi-quantitative/quantitative approaches) is the direct process step — sub-clause number consistent with project convention. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.3 'Analysing information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

§8a Abs.1 'angemessene' measures implies evaluation of which risks exceed acceptable levels; ISO 27005 Cl.7.4 (risk evaluation — comparing risk analysis results against risk acceptance criteria) is the direct decision step — sub-clause number consistent with project convention. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.4 'Evaluating the information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

§8a Abs.1a Satz 1 mandates mandatory deployment of Systeme zur Angriffserkennung (SzA) — attack detection systems — as a specific statutory control; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 covers risk identification as the process step that justifies selection of detective controls, but 27005 does not mandate any specific control type — BSIG therefore goes beyond 27005 in prescribing the control. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

§8a Abs.1a Satz 1 prescribes attack-detection systems as a mandatory risk treatment control; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 provides the risk treatment process (identify options, select controls, plan implementation) but does not mandate specific controls — BSIG prescribes the concrete output of the 27005 treatment selection step.

§8a Abs.1a Satz 2 requires SzA to continuously and automatically capture and evaluate parameters and characteristics from live operations; ISO 27005 Cl.9.1 (monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation) covers the generic monitoring process but BSIG prescribes specific technical requirements (continuous, automated, from live operations) that exceed the process-level 27005 guidance. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

§8a Abs.1a Satz 2 requires SzA to evaluate captured data to detect anomalies and attacks continuously; ISO 27005 Cl.9.1 covers monitoring effectiveness of risk treatment but does not reach down to the technical requirement of real-time anomaly detection — BSIG operationalises the monitoring output at a prescriptive technical level. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

§8a Abs.1a Satz 3 implicitly requires logging as a prerequisite of SzA functionality; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 identifies controls to treat risks but logging as a specific technical safeguard is not enumerated — BSIG prescribes the control; 27005 provides only the treatment selection framework.

§8a Abs.1a Satz 3 requires SzA to also support response to detected attacks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 treatment includes controls selection but does not prescribe incident-response capability as a mandatory output — BSIG extends the treatment obligation into post-detection operational response.

The BSI Orientierungshilfe SzA (OH SzA) operationalises §8a Abs.1a with mandatory requirements for logging, detection and response tasks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 provides the risk treatment process framework that legitimises control selection but the OH SzA goes to prescriptive technical detail (log retention periods, SIEM requirements) well beyond what 27005 specifies.

§8a Abs.2 allows sector-specific security standards (B3S) approved by BSI to substitute or supplement state-of-the-art requirements; ISO 27005 Cl.6.2 (interested-party requirements) is the mechanism by which regulatory and sector-specific standards are identified as context constraints — B3S standards represent a specific category of interested-party requirements captured in this clause.

B3S standards establish sector-specific risk criteria and baseline security measures; ISO 27005 Cl.6.3 requires organisations to establish and maintain risk criteria — B3S risk profiles from the sector associations feed directly into the risk criteria establishment activity.

§8a Abs.3 Satz 1 requires KRITIS operators to provide documented proof of compliance every two years; ISO 27005 Cl.10.4.3 (documented information about results) requires maintaining documented information about risk assessment and treatment results — the biennial proof obligation consumes the documented outputs mandated by 10.4.3.

§8a Abs.3 Satz 2 permits security audits, reviews or certifications as proof forms; ISO 27005 Cl.9.2 (internal audit of the risk management process — consistent with project NIS2 mapping conventions) covers periodic review of the risk management process — the BSIG audit requirement is a compliance-audit overlay on the ISO 27005 process-review activity. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.2 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

§8a Abs.3 Satz 3 explicitly requires the audit report to contain a risk analysis; ISO 27005 Cl.7 (information security risk assessment process) is the direct process that produces the risk analysis documentation — the statutory audit-report requirement directly consumes 27005 Cl.7 outputs.

§8a Abs.3 Satz 3 requires the audit report to assess technical and organisational measures (TOM) taken; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 covers the risk treatment process, including identification and selection of controls — the TOM assessment in the audit report directly evaluates the outputs of the Cl.8.6 treatment planning.

§8a Abs.3 Satz 3 requires the audit report to include recommendations for remedying deficiencies; ISO 27005 Cl.9.3 (management review — consistent with project conventions) covers review outputs including decisions and improvement directives — the deficiency remediation recommendations are the corrective-action outputs of the management review step. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.3 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.7 'Corrective action'.]

§8a Abs.3 requires submission of audit results to the BSI; ISO 27005 Cl.10.4.3 mandates retention of documented risk management results — the submission obligation presupposes the documented outputs that Cl.10.4.3 requires be maintained.

§8a Abs.4 grants the BSI the right to verify that KRITIS operators comply with §8a Abs.1; ISO 27005 Cl.9.2 (internal audit) covers planned review of risk management process adequacy — the BSI verification right is an external supervisory audit that consumes evidence from the same 27005 audit/review process. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.2 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

§8a Abs.5 authorises the BSI to specify procedural requirements for audit conduct, evidence documents and auditor qualifications; ISO 27005 Cl.10.4.2 (documented information about processes) requires maintaining documented information describing the risk management process — BSI procedural specifications define what documented process information must demonstrate.

BSI KdA Requirement Category 'Management' requires KRITIS operators to establish an ISMS aligned to established standards; ISO 27005 Cl.5 defines the information security risk management overview process that sits within an ISMS — the KdA ISMS requirement is a governance container for the 27005 risk process.

BSI KdA explicitly requires operators to implement a documented information security risk management process as part of their ISMS; ISO 27005 Cl.5 defines exactly this process — the KdA requirement is a statutory reference to the 27005 process model.

BSI KdA requires operators to define and document the scope of their ISMS/risk management covering KRITIS-relevant IT systems and processes; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 (organisational context and scope) directly specifies the scope-establishment activity for the risk management process.

BSI KdA requires consideration of legal, regulatory and contractual requirements (including BSIG, KritisV, sector laws) as risk context inputs; ISO 27005 Cl.6.2 (identifying basic requirements of interested parties) directly captures this obligation as the activity of identifying and recording requirements from all relevant stakeholders.

BSI KdA requires operators to define risk acceptance criteria and assessment methodology consistent with the state-of-the-art requirement; ISO 27005 Cl.6.3 directly requires establishment and maintenance of risk acceptance criteria and criteria for performing risk assessments — a direct process equivalence.

BSI KdA requires a Schutzbedarfsfeststellung (protection-requirements determination) for KRITIS assets identifying impact on VAIC protection goals; ISO 27005 Cl.6.4.3.2 requires definition of consequence criteria aligned to CIA security properties — the BSI Schutzbedarfsfeststellung is the specific instantiation of this consequence-criteria definition activity.

BSI KdA requires KRITIS operators to maintain a complete inventory of IT systems, components and processes within scope; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 includes identification of assets as part of organisational context — the asset inventory is a sub-element of the broader context-establishment activity.

BSI KdA requires a documented threat analysis (Bedrohungsanalyse) identifying relevant threat scenarios for KRITIS installations; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 (risk identification) covers identification of risk sources, threat agents and threat events as the first step of risk identification — exact sub-clause number consistent with project convention. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA Bedrohungsanalyse requires specific attention to physical, cyber and hybrid threat scenarios relevant to critical infrastructure; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 covers general risk scenario identification across all risk types — the KRITIS threat-scenario focus is a sector-specific narrowing of the broader 27005 identification scope. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires a vulnerability analysis (Schwachstellenanalyse) identifying weaknesses in IT systems and processes; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 in the asset-based approach covers identification of vulnerabilities as a component of risk identification — the Schwachstellenanalyse is the operationalisation of the vulnerability-identification sub-step. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA risk analysis requires estimation of likelihood (Eintrittswahrscheinlichkeit) for each identified threat/vulnerability pair; ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 (risk analysis) directly requires estimation of likelihood as a core analytical activity — exact sub-clause consistent with project convention. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.3 'Analysing information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA risk analysis requires assessment of potential damage/impact (Schadensausmaß) for each risk scenario; ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 requires estimation of consequence/impact as the other core dimension of risk analysis alongside likelihood. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.3 'Analysing information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires risk evaluation (Risikobeurteilung) comparing analysed risks against defined acceptance criteria to prioritise treatment; ISO 27005 Cl.7.4 (risk evaluation) is the direct process step requiring comparison of risk analysis results with risk criteria and prioritisation for treatment. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.4 'Evaluating the information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires a documented risk treatment plan (Risikobehandlungsplan) selecting and scheduling measures for unacceptable risks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 covers the information security risk treatment process including identification, selection and planning of treatment options — a direct process equivalence.

BSI KdA requires selected measures to be proportionate to the assessed risk level (state-of-the-art proportionality); ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 includes evaluation of the risk treatment options against risk criteria established in Cl.6.3 — the proportionality check is built into the treatment selection activity.

BSI KdA requires assessment and acceptance of residual risk after treatment measures are selected; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6.3 (Acceptance of the residual information security risk) directly governs formal evaluation and acceptance of the remaining risk post-treatment — an exact functional equivalence.

BSI KdA requires communication of risk assessment results and treatment decisions to relevant internal stakeholders including management; ISO 27005 Cl.10.3 (risk communication and consultation — consistent with project NIS2 mapping conventions) covers sharing of risk information with interested parties — exact sub-clause number consistent with project convention. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 10.3 'Communication and consultation' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires continuous monitoring of the risk situation, including new threats and changes to KRITIS systems; ISO 27005 Cl.9.1 (monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation) covers the ongoing surveillance of risks and risk management process effectiveness — exact sub-clause consistent with project convention. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

BSI KdA requires periodic (at minimum two-yearly, aligned with the §8a Abs.3 proof cycle) review of the risk management process and results; ISO 27005 Cl.9.2 (internal audit/review) covers planned review of the adequacy and effectiveness of the risk management process — exact sub-clause consistent with project convention. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.2 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

BSI KdA requires senior management to review risk management results and approve residual risk acceptance; ISO 27005 Cl.9.3 (management review — consistent with project convention) covers management's review of risk management system performance and outputs — an exact governance equivalence. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.3 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.6 'Management review'.]

BSI KdA requires documented procedures and processes for all risk management activities; ISO 27005 Cl.10.4.2 (documented information about processes) directly mandates maintaining documented information that describes the risk management process.

BSI KdA requires documented outputs of risk assessment (risk register, treatment plan, residual risk acceptance records) to be maintained as audit evidence; ISO 27005 Cl.10.4.3 (documented information about results) directly mandates retention of risk management results documentation.

BSI KdA requires a Business Impact Analysis (BIA) as part of BCM to quantify impact of KRITIS service disruptions; ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 risk analysis covers impact/consequence estimation — the BIA is a deeper, availability-focused consequence analysis that partially overlaps with but extends the 27005 impact estimation scope. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.3 'Analysing information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires BCM emergency plans as a risk treatment measure for availability risks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 includes business continuity as a risk treatment option — BCM planning is a specific treatment pathway captured within the treatment process, but BCM implementation requirements (ISO 22301) go substantially beyond what 27005 specifies.

BSI KdA requires supplier and service-provider risk management including assessment of third-party IT risks to KRITIS installations; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 treatment covers risk sharing/transfer options including third-party measures, but supply-chain risk management details (contractual requirements, audit rights) exceed the 27005 treatment planning scope.

BSI KdA requires identification of risks arising from suppliers and sub-contractors as a risk identification sub-activity; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 covers all risk identification including supply-chain scenarios — the KRITIS supply-chain focus is a specific sub-scope within the broader 27005 risk identification perimeter. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires physical security risks (perimeter, access control, environmental threats) to be included in risk identification; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 covers all risk sources including physical/environmental — the physical risk scope is a sub-element of the broader 27005 risk identification coverage. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires personnel security risks (insider threats, access management failures) to be identified; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 covers human-threat sources including malicious insiders and unintentional errors — personnel risk identification is a sub-element of the comprehensive 27005 risk identification scope. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires risk analysis to cover both OT (operational technology, ICS/SCADA) and IT systems in KRITIS installations; ISO 27005 Cl.6.1 establishes the scope of the risk management context — but 27005 is information-security-centric and does not explicitly address OT/ICS risk scenarios, which are a KRITIS-specific extension of the standard IT scope.

BSI KdA OT/IT risk identification must address SCADA, ICS and cyber-physical system vulnerabilities specific to critical infrastructure; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 covers technical asset and vulnerability identification but lacks guidance on OT-specific threat modelling (safety-system interactions, cascade failures) — BSIG extends the 27005 identification scope into the OT domain. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires cryptographic strength to be assessed as part of confidentiality/integrity risk analysis; ISO 27005 Cl.7.3 consequence estimation covers all dimensions of risk impact including cryptographic compromise — the crypto-risk assessment is a specialised sub-element of the broader 27005 impact analysis. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.3 'Analysing information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires vulnerability tracking and patch management as part of ongoing vulnerability identification; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 asset-based approach includes vulnerability identification as a risk identification activity — patch management operationalises the continuous re-identification of vulnerabilities required by the 27005 iterative process. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA requires IT emergency management (IT-Notfallmanagement) as a risk treatment measure for high-availability risks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 includes continuity/recovery measures as a treatment option class — IT emergency management is one specific implementation of the risk modification/reduction treatment option.

BSI KdA requires security awareness training as an organisational risk treatment measure for human-factor risks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 includes organisational controls (including awareness) as treatment options — awareness training is a specific preventive treatment measure within the broader 27005 treatment catalogue.

BSI KdA requires network segmentation (especially OT/IT separation) as a mandatory risk treatment measure for lateral movement risks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 covers technical controls as treatment options — network segmentation is a specific technical risk-modification treatment within the 27005 treatment selection framework.

BSI KdA requires access management (identity verification, least privilege) as a risk treatment measure for unauthorised-access risks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 covers technical and organisational controls selection — access controls are a specific preventive treatment measure within the treatment options framework.

BSI KdA requires documented incident reporting and escalation paths including mandatory BSI notification under §8b; ISO 27005 Cl.9.1 monitoring covers identification of risk events and anomalies — incident reporting is downstream of the monitoring activity but the statutory reporting obligation extends beyond the 27005 monitoring process scope. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.1 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

BSI KdA requires the risk management process to be subject to continual improvement, with management review outputs driving updates to the risk programme; ISO 27005 Cl.9.3 (management review) covers review outputs including improvement decisions that feed back into the risk management cycle. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.3 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.8 'Continual improvement'.]

BSI KdA requires corrective and improvement actions arising from audits, incidents and management reviews to be documented and tracked; ISO 27005 Cl.10.2 (consistent with project convention for continual improvement) covers nonconformity and corrective action as part of the improvement process — exact sub-clause consistent with project convention. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.10.2 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.7 'Corrective action'.]

BSI KdA requires maintenance of a risk register documenting all identified risks, their assessed levels, treatment status and residual risk; ISO 27005 Cl.10.4.3 (documented information about results) requires retention of risk assessment and treatment results — the risk register is the primary document operationalising this clause.

BSI KdA audit evidence requires a Statement of Applicability (SoA) or equivalent showing which controls are applied and their implementation status relative to identified risks; ISO 27005 Cl.8.4 (consistent with Secureframe/PECB sources describing SoA-related provisions) covers the applicability statement for selected controls — exact sub-clause consistent with sources. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.8.4 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 8.5 'Producing a Statement of Applicability'.]

BSI KdA requires risks to be prioritised for treatment based on their assessed risk level (state-of-the-art proportionality); ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 includes prioritisation of risks for treatment as an inherent part of the treatment planning process — direct functional equivalence.

BSI KdA permits risk sharing/transfer (e.g. cyber insurance, outsourcing to certified providers) as a treatment option; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 explicitly includes risk sharing as one of the four treatment options (avoidance, modification, retention, sharing).

BSI KdA allows risk retention/acceptance for low residual risks as a documented treatment decision; ISO 27005 Cl.8.6 includes risk retention as one of the four treatment options, with formal acceptance documented per Cl.8.6.3.

BSI KdA threat scenarios use an event-based approach identifying risk sources and attack chains relevant to KRITIS sector; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 explicitly describes the event-based risk identification approach as one of two canonical approaches — exact functional equivalence for the KRITIS event/scenario-based threat modelling. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI KdA Schwachstellenanalyse uses an asset-based approach identifying specific technical vulnerabilities per system component; ISO 27005 Cl.7.2 also explicitly describes the asset-based approach — the KdA vulnerability analysis methodology is the direct operational instantiation of the 27005 asset-based risk identification approach. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.2 'Identifying information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]

BSI RUN (Reife- und Umsetzungsgradbewertung, mandatory from 2025) assesses maturity of ISMS and risk management implementation across five maturity grades; ISO 27005 Cl.9.2 covers review of the risk management process adequacy — the RUN maturity assessment is a structured external evaluation of the risk management process quality, extending the 27005 review with a prescriptive 5-level maturity scale. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): Sonnet had Cl.9.2 assuming ISO 27001-aligned numbering, but in 27005:2022 this concept maps to 10.5 'Monitoring and review'.]

BSI KdA BCM risk evaluation requires prioritising continuity risks based on their criticality to KRITIS service delivery; ISO 27005 Cl.7.4 (risk evaluation) covers prioritisation of risks against criteria including business impact — the BCM-specific risk prioritisation is a sub-activity of the broader 27005 evaluation step focused on availability impact. [Verified against ISO/IEC 27005:2022 official Contents (ISO sample): 7.4 'Evaluating the information security risks' confirmed; UNVERIFIED flag removed.]
